frameworks.name: signal detection theory / metacognition (type-2 sensitivity)
frameworks.one_line: Metacognitive awareness operationalised as type-2 detection of one's own perceptual accuracy.

frameworks.name: Bayesian inference over competing experimental outcomes
frameworks.one_line: Combines a positive and a negative result to estimate posterior probability of a true TMS effect.

frameworks.name: Monte Carlo simulation of subject-exclusion criteria
frameworks.one_line: Computer simulations test whether exclusion rules reduce false positives as claimed.

core_question: Does continuous theta-burst TMS to DLPFC genuinely impair visual metacognition, given a failed replication that relied on post-hoc subject exclusion?
key_assumptions: Original Rounis et al. effect on type-2 metacognition is the reference phenomenon.
Bor et al.'s exclusion criteria were intended to reduce false positives.
Positive and negative outcomes from the same study can be jointly evaluated in a Bayesian framework.
manipulations: Re-analysis of Bor et al.'s pre- vs post-exclusion samples.
Simulated datasets under null and alternative hypotheses to evaluate exclusion rules.
key_findings: Bor et al. obtained an effect similar to Rounis et al. before applying exclusion.
Simulations show their exclusion criteria do not actually reduce false-positive rates.
Combining the positive (pre-exclusion) and negative (post-exclusion) results yields >=75% posterior probability that DLPFC TMS impairs metacognition.
interpretations: The 'failed' replication is better read as evidence consistent with a real TMS-on-metacognition effect.
Subject-exclusion practices need formal validation rather than intuitive justification.
